briefs.title: Proposed: Burnaby Bylaw Would Change How Advisory Body Rules Are Set and Updated
briefs.summary: Council is set to vote on a bylaw amendment that would change who controls the rules — called 'Terms of Reference' — for the City's advisory bodies (committees that advise Council). Under the proposed change, the Mayor would direct staff to prepare these rules, and both the Mayor and the Chief Administrative Officer could request updates, but all changes would still go through Council for final approval.
briefs.action_required: Watch for the final adoption vote — this affects how Burnaby advisory bodies operate and are governed.

key_dates.label: Burnaby Procedure Bylaw 2024 Amendment No.1 — final adoption vote

briefs.title: Proposed: Burnaby to Cut Plumbing Permit Fees and Offer Refunds for Some Developments
briefs.summary: Council is set to vote on a bylaw that would temporarily reduce plumbing permit fees for most developments (excluding small-scale multi-unit housing and tenant improvements). Under the proposal, the fee for a first fixture would drop from $121 to $70, and each additional fixture from $67 to $39 — for permits issued on or before June 30, 2026. Owners who already paid the higher rate between January 1 and March 10, 2026 could receive a refund for the difference.
briefs.action_required: If you pulled a plumbing permit in early 2026, check if you qualify for a refund once this bylaw is adopted.

key_dates.label: Burnaby reduced plumbing permit fee window closes — permits must be issued by this date
Burnaby plumbing permit refund eligibility window — permits issued in this period qualify

briefs.title: Proposed: Burnaby Bylaw Would Set $500 Fines for Bear Spray Sales Violations
briefs.summary: Council is set to vote on a proposed bylaw that would add $500 fines for businesses that break rules around selling bear spray — for example, selling to anyone under 19, failing to check photo ID, or not keeping proper sales records. No discounts or reduced penalties would be available for these fines.
briefs.action_required: If you sell bear spray, review the proposed storage, ID-check, and record-keeping rules now to prepare for compliance.

briefs.title: Proposed: Burnaby Bylaw Would Restrict Who Can Sell Bear Spray
briefs.summary: Council is set to vote on a proposed bylaw amendment that would limit bear spray sales in Burnaby to outdoor and sporting goods retailers only. If approved, these sellers would need to check photo ID, refuse sales to anyone under 19, keep bear spray locked up, and maintain detailed sales records for at least 12 months.
briefs.action_required: If you sell bear spray, check whether your business qualifies as an 'authorized vendor' under the proposed rules.

key_dates.label: Burnaby Business Licence Bear Spray Amendment — council adoption vote

briefs.title: Council to vote on final approval of mid-rise rental building at 5889 Barker Avenue, Burnaby
briefs.summary: Burnaby Council is set to vote on the final adoption of a rezoning bylaw (Bylaw 14782, Amendment No. 19) that would allow a mid-rise rental apartment building at 5889 Barker Avenue in the Metrotown area. The project, proposed by Concord Greenhouse (Nominee) Ltd., would include 106 rental units — 48 of which would be non-market (below-market-rate) rentals.
briefs.action_required: If this development affects your business or property nearby, attend the March 10, 2026 Council meeting to have your say.

key_dates.label: Burnaby REZ 25-15 rezoning bylaw — Council final adoption vote

briefs.industries: Construction & Development, Landlord & Property Mgmt, Tenant

briefs.title: Proposed: Burnaby Council to vote on rezoning amendment for Barker + Olive development
briefs.summary: Council is set to vote on a proposed bylaw that would amend Burnaby's zoning map to allow changes to a development called 'Barker + Olive.' The rezoning — a change to the rules about how a specific piece of land can be used — would update the approved development plan for that site, with the final adoption expected in early 2026.
briefs.action_required: Watch for the council adoption vote in early 2026 if this rezoning affects your property or business location.

key_dates.label: Burnaby Zoning Amendment Bylaw No. 14782 — third reading passed
Burnaby Zoning Amendment Bylaw No. 14782 — final adoption vote

briefs.title: Council to vote on final approval of six-storey self-storage facility at Still Creek Avenue
briefs.summary: Burnaby Council is set to vote on the final adoption of a rezoning bylaw (Bylaw 14763) that would allow a six-storey self-storage building with underground parking to be built at 3942–3944 Still Creek Avenue. The applicant has met all required conditions set by the City, and the Planning Department is recommending Council give the bylaw its final approval at the March 10, 2026 meeting.
briefs.action_required: Watch the March 10, 2026 Council meeting if this development affects your business or property.

key_dates.label: Burnaby REZ 25-05 rezoning bylaw final adoption vote — Council meeting

briefs.industries: Construction & Development, Transportation & Logistics, Landlord & Property Mgmt

briefs.title: Proposed: Burnaby Council to vote on rezoning for self-storage facility at Still Creek
briefs.summary: Council is set to vote on a proposed bylaw that would rezone two properties at 3942 and 3944 Still Creek in Burnaby to allow for an amended self-storage development. The rezoning — a change to the rules about how land can be used — would update an existing plan to reflect new building designs prepared by Studio One Architecture Inc.
briefs.action_required: Watch for the final council adoption vote in 2026 — this could affect nearby property and business owners.

key_dates.label: Burnaby Zoning Bylaw Amendment No. 14, 2025 — final adoption vote

briefs.title: Council to vote on final approval of rental tower and community hub in Edmonds
briefs.summary: Burnaby City Council is set to vote on the final adoption of a rezoning bylaw (Bylaw 14744) that would allow a residential rental tower with a community hub to be built at 7135 Walker Ave. and 7244 Arcola St. in the Edmonds neighbourhood. The applicant, Gracorp Properties LP, has met all the conditions set by the City, and Council will consider giving the bylaw its final approval at the March 10, 2026 meeting.
briefs.action_required: Watch the March 10, 2026 Council meeting — this rezoning decision could affect nearby businesses.

key_dates.label: Burnaby REZ 24-20 rezoning bylaw final adoption vote — Council meeting

briefs.industries: Construction & Development, Landlord & Property Mgmt, Tenant

briefs.title: Proposed: Burnaby Rezoning of Two Properties on Walker and Arcola Streets
briefs.summary: Council is set to vote on Bylaw No. 14744, which would rezone two properties — 7135 Walker Street and 7244 Arcola Street — in Burnaby under a new Comprehensive Development (CD) zone. A proposed bylaw would change how these lands can be used and built on, with any future development required to follow attached design and use guidelines.
briefs.action_required: Watch for the final council adoption vote — this rezoning could affect nearby property use and development plans.

key_dates.label: Burnaby Zoning Bylaw Amendment No. 14744 — final adoption vote scheduled

briefs.title: Proposed: Burnaby Zoning Bylaw Amendment to Create New Employment, Industrial, and Agricultural Districts
briefs.summary: Council is set to vote on Bylaw No. 14792, which would update Burnaby's zoning rules by creating several new land-use zones — including an Employment District (E1), Industrial District (I1), Interim Parks/Open Spaces District (PM1), Interim Institutional District (PM2), and Interim Agricultural District (AM1). The proposed bylaw would also add or update dozens of definitions (such as 'light industrial,' 'commissary kitchen,' and 'works yard') and adjust rules around building height, fencing, parking, home-based businesses, and outdoor storage.
briefs.action_required: Check if your property or business location falls under a zone that may be renamed or redefined — this could affect what you are allowed to do there.

key_dates.label: Burnaby Zoning Bylaw Amendment No. 2, 2026 — third reading scheduled

briefs.industries: Construction & Development, Agriculture, Landlord & Property Mgmt

briefs.title: Proposed: Burnaby Council to vote on energy-based fees for public EV chargers
briefs.summary: Council is set to vote on switching Burnaby's public EV charging stations from time-based fees (paying by the hour) to energy-based fees (paying by the kilowatt-hour of electricity actually used). If approved, Level 2 chargers would cost $0.2865/kWh and Level 3 chargers $0.3479/kWh, plus idle fees for vehicles that stay plugged in after a full charge.
briefs.action_required: Watch for the council decision — new EV charging rates could affect your business fleet or employee parking costs.

key_dates.label: Burnaby energy-based EV charging fees — 30-day public notice period begins after Council approval

briefs.title: Proposed: New Fine for Exceeding Time Limit at EV Charging Parking Spaces in Burnaby
briefs.summary: Council is set to vote on a bylaw amendment that would add a new fine for drivers who stay too long in an electric vehicle (EV) metered parking space. Under the proposal, overstaying the maximum time limit at an EV charging meter would result in a $75 fine (reduced to $60 if paid early).
briefs.action_required: Watch for the council decision — this could affect staff or customers who park at EV charging meters.

briefs.title: Proposed: Burnaby to update EV charging fees at city-owned parking stations
briefs.summary: Council is set to vote on a bylaw that would replace the current EV charging fee structure at city-owned stations with two new rate tables. One table sets energy-based rates (charged per kilowatt-hour used), and the other sets time-based rates (charged by how long you park). The proposed rates range from $0.2865/kWh for Level 2 chargers to $0.3479/kWh for Level 3 chargers, plus idle fees if you stay past your charge.
briefs.action_required: Check if your business fleet uses Burnaby city EV chargers — new rates could affect your operating costs.

briefs.title: Proposed: Burnaby to update EV charging meter rules and fee structures
briefs.summary: Council is set to vote on a bylaw amendment that would update how electric vehicle (EV) charging meters work in Burnaby. The proposed changes would allow fees to be charged either by time or by energy used, update the technical definition of EV charging meters to include faster Level 3 chargers (up to 350 kW), and set a maximum time limit for how long a vehicle can occupy an EV charging space.
briefs.action_required: Watch for the council vote — this could affect you if you operate or use public EV charging spaces in Burnaby.

briefs.title: Council to Consider: Burnaby RCMP Status Report on Crime and Policing, August–November 2025
briefs.summary: Burnaby City Council is set to receive for information the Burnaby RCMP's status update covering August to November 2025, along with a quarterly performance report for July to September 2025. The report outlines policing activity across the city, including drug seizures, robbery investigations, traffic enforcement, and community outreach — giving Council a picture of public safety trends and how the RCMP is meeting its annual goals.
briefs.action_required: Review the crime trends in your area — especially robbery and theft from vehicles — and consider updating your security measures.

briefs.title: Council to Consider: Burnaby Fire Department Status Update for December 2025
briefs.summary: Council is set to receive a status update report from the Burnaby Fire Department covering activities through the end of December 2025. The report covers emergency response statistics, fire prevention inspections, training hours, and community outreach — it is being presented for information only and does not propose any new rules or requirements for businesses.
briefs.action_required: No action needed — this is an information-only report with no new rules or deadlines for business owners.

briefs.title: Council to consider status update on Burnaby–New Westminster Youth Exploitation Task Force
briefs.summary: Council is set to receive a status update on the Burnaby–New Westminster Task Force on Sexually Exploited and At-Risk Youth. The report covers the Task Force's recent activities — including a November 2025 professional development day on online sexual exploitation — and outlines plans to continue meetings and workshops in 2026. No new funding or bylaw changes are proposed at this time.

briefs.title: Council to Consider Updates on Major Burnaby Recreation and Civic Building Projects
briefs.summary: Burnaby City Council is set to receive status updates on seven major civic construction projects, including a new RCMP building, three new recreation centres, and a theatre renewal. Council will also consider a staffing and operations plan — called 'People-Play' — to prepare for opening the new recreation facilities, which will nearly double the city's recreation services by 2030.
briefs.action_required: Watch for Council's budget decisions in late 2026 — new facilities may affect local labour demand and business opportunities.

key_dates.label: Mintara Restaurant expansion handover to Parks, Recreation and Culture — Q3 2026
People-Play phased launch scenarios presented to Council — late 2026
James Cowan Theatre handover to Parks, Recreation and Culture — Q1 2027
Community Safety Building (RCMP detachment) construction completion — Q4 2027
Burnaby Lake Recreation Centre handover to Parks, Recreation and Culture — Q1 2028
Cameron Community Centre and Library handover to Parks, Recreation and Culture — Q2 2028
Brentwood Community Centre construction completion — Q1 2030

briefs.industries: Construction & Development, Labour & Employment, Food Service

briefs.title: Council to Consider: Status Update on Burnaby's 2025 Casino Gaming Reserve Spending
briefs.summary: Burnaby City Council is set to receive a status update on the Gaming Reserve — a special fund fed by 10% of net casino proceeds from Grand Villa Casino. The report shows the reserve received about $10.1M in casino grants in 2025, spent roughly $12.7M on city programs and projects, and ended the year with a balance of about $11.7M.
briefs.action_required: No action required — this is an information-only report with no direct impact on business owners.

briefs.title: Council to Consider: Status Update on Burnaby Mountain Safety Reserve Spending
briefs.summary: Burnaby City Council is set to receive a status update on the Burnaby Mountain Safety Reserve — a fund built from a $20.1 million agreement with Trans Mountain Corporation paid out over 21 years. So far, $6.7 million has been spent on three projects: an alternate access route on Burnaby Mountain, a hazardous materials response vehicle, and operating costs for Fire Station 8.
briefs.action_required: No action required — this is an information-only update with no direct impact on business owners.
briefs.industries: Transportation & Logistics, Natural Resources, Construction & Development

briefs.title: Council to Consider Supporting Tourism Burnaby's Bid to Host 2028 Urban Forest Conference
briefs.summary: Council is set to vote on supporting Tourism Burnaby's plan to submit a bid to host the 2028 Canadian Urban Forest Conference. This is a 'support in principle' only — meaning the City would back the bid effort but is not being asked to contribute any money at this stage. If approved, staff would later report back on what resources and planning would be needed.
briefs.action_required: Watch for the council decision — it could bring a major national conference and visitors to Burnaby in 2028.

key_dates.label: Tourism Burnaby — 2028 CUFC bid proposal submission deadline

briefs.industries: Hotel & Accommodation, Food Service, Transportation & Logistics

briefs.title: Council to vote on: Burnaby's new 25-year Parks, Recreation and Culture Plan
briefs.summary: Burnaby City Council is set to vote on approving the draft 'Making Life Better: Parks, Recreation and Culture Plan' — a 25-year roadmap for parks, recreation facilities, and cultural services across the city. If approved, the plan would also authorize a second round of public engagement this spring, where residents and businesses can weigh in before the final version is brought back to Council for adoption in late spring 2026.
briefs.action_required: Participate in the upcoming public engagement sessions this spring to share your feedback before the plan is finalized.

key_dates.label: Burnaby PRC Plan — Council approval vote at regular meeting
Burnaby PRC Plan — second round public engagement opens
Burnaby PRC Plan — public engagement findings reported to PRCC
Burnaby PRC Plan — final plan expected at Council for approval

briefs.title: Proposed: Burnaby Council to vote on Community Grant Policy and application updates
briefs.summary: Burnaby City Council is set to vote on changes to its Community Grant Policy and application form. The proposed updates would clarify what grant money cannot be used for (such as scholarships, conference travel, or debt repayment), require Burnaby-based non-profits to show a Burnaby address, remove one-time events from Food Security grant eligibility, and add a new question asking how many Burnaby residents a program serves each month.
briefs.action_required: If you run a non-profit in Burnaby, review the draft policy changes before the March 10, 2026 Council vote.

key_dates.label: Burnaby Community Grant Policy amendments — Council vote

briefs.title: Council to vote on: Burnaby IT ticketing software contract worth $580,985
briefs.summary: Burnaby City Council is set to vote on awarding a three-year software licence contract to ServiceNow, Inc. for approximately $580,985 (including taxes), running from March 16, 2026 to March 15, 2029. This contract would cover the licences needed to keep the City's internal IT help-desk system running — the tool city staff use to submit and track IT requests.
briefs.action_required: Watch for the council decision on March 10, 2026 — this is an internal city IT contract with no direct impact on most businesses.

key_dates.label: Burnaby ServiceNow IT licence contract start date — if approved

briefs.title: Council to Consider Applying for Up to $200,000 in Provincial Development Approvals Grant
briefs.summary: Burnaby Council is set to vote on whether to apply for up to $200,000 in provincial grant funding through the UBCM Local Government Development Approvals Program (LGDAP). If approved, the money would help the City speed up building permits and development approvals — especially for small-scale housing, modular construction, and electrical service connections — making it faster and easier to get projects approved.
briefs.action_required: Watch for the council decision — faster permit approvals could affect your development or construction timelines.

briefs.title: Council to vote on granting BC Hydro land access at Burnaby's new Community Safety Building
briefs.summary: Burnaby Council is set to vote on allowing BC Hydro to use a small portion of the city-owned land at 4111 Norland Avenue. This access — called a Statutory Right-of-Way (meaning a legal permission to use someone else's land for a specific purpose) — would let BC Hydro install, operate, and maintain the electrical infrastructure needed to power the new Community Safety Building.

key_dates.label: Burnaby Council vote — BC Hydro right-of-way at 4111 Norland Avenue

briefs.title: Council to vote on sending concerns about proposed Metro 2050 boundary expansion options
briefs.summary: Burnaby Council is set to vote on sending a formal letter to the Metro Vancouver Board raising concerns about four proposed changes to Metro 2050 — the regional plan that guides where growth and development can happen across Metro Vancouver. The proposed changes, requested by the mayors of Surrey, Langley Township, and Delta, would make it easier to expand the Urban Containment Boundary (the line that separates urban development from rural and agricultural land), and would lower the level of regional agreement needed to approve those expansions.
briefs.action_required: Submit comments to Metro Vancouver before March 20, 2026 if this proposal could affect your business or property.

key_dates.label: Metro 2050 amendment options comment period closes — Metro Vancouver
Metro Vancouver Board considers initial readings of amendment bylaws
Metro Vancouver Board considers final reading and adoption of amendment bylaw

briefs.industries: Construction & Development, Landlord & Property Mgmt, Transportation & Logistics

briefs.title: Council to vote on liquor licence for Solo Karaoke on Kingsway, Burnaby
briefs.summary: Burnaby Council is set to vote on whether to recommend a Liquor Primary Licence — a permit that allows a business to serve alcohol as its main activity — for Solo Karaoke at 102–6462 Kingsway. City staff, the RCMP, and the provincial liquor regulator investigated earlier concerns and found no evidence of illegal liquor service, so staff are now recommending Council support the application.
briefs.action_required: Watch for the Council decision on March 10, 2026 — it could affect nearby businesses or your own licence.

key_dates.label: Burnaby Council vote — Solo Karaoke liquor primary licence recommendation

briefs.title: Council to Consider: Solo Karaoke Ltd. Delegation Request in Burnaby
briefs.summary: Burnaby City Council is set to hear a delegation request from Solo Karaoke Ltd., meaning the business has asked for time to speak directly to Council at an upcoming meeting. Council will consider what the business has to say before any related decision is made. The outcome could affect how solo karaoke businesses operate or are permitted in Burnaby.
briefs.action_required: Watch for the Council decision — it could affect licensing or operating rules for entertainment businesses in Burnaby.

key_dates.label: Solo Karaoke Ltd. delegation — Burnaby Council meeting date

briefs.title: Proposed: Burnaby to Regulate Bear Spray Sales and Raise Parking Fees
briefs.summary: At its February 24, 2026 meeting, Burnaby Council voted on several items affecting local businesses, including proposed bylaw amendments that would regulate who can sell bear spray, a plumbing permit fee increase, higher on-street parking rates, and the renewal of the North Road Business Improvement Area for 2026–2031. These measures have passed initial readings but some still require final bylaw adoption before they take effect.
briefs.action_required: Check if your business sells bear spray — new licence rules and fines may apply once the bylaw is adopted.

key_dates.label: Burnaby North Road BIA renewal bylaw — first, second and third reading passed
Burnaby bear spray sales regulation bylaw — first, second and third reading passed
Burnaby on-street public pay parking fee rate increase — approved
Burnaby plumbing permit fee amendment bylaw — first, second and third reading passed
Burnaby outdoor special events at Deer Lake, Central Park, Swangard — season opens
Burnaby outdoor special events at Deer Lake, Central Park, Swangard — season closes

briefs.industries: Retail, Construction & Development, Landlord & Property Mgmt

briefs.title: Proposed: Burnaby to Replace Entire Zoning Bylaw, Effective July 1, 2026
briefs.summary: Burnaby City Council is set to consider a full replacement of its Zoning Bylaw — the rulebook that controls what can be built and where. The proposed new bylaw would introduce clearer rules on building size, parking, landscaping, and minimum unit sizes, and is expected to come into effect on July 1, 2026.
briefs.action_required: Review the proposed Zoning Bylaw changes before the April/May introduction — they may affect your property or business.

key_dates.label: Burnaby new Zoning Bylaw introduced to Council — first reading
Burnaby new Zoning Bylaw adoption by Council
Burnaby new Zoning Bylaw takes effect

briefs.industries: Construction & Development, Landlord & Property Mgmt, Tenant
